note: First-pass bank authored from current Angular/FE interview canon (Angular 17-20 era). Sources tag = 'canon' for now; specific URLs to be added via per-topic research agents in future passes.

questions.question: Which best describes the difference between signal() and computed() in Angular?

options.text: signal() is writable; computed() is derived, lazy, and memoized.
computed() recomputes on every change detection tick.
signal() requires NgZone; computed() does not.
They are aliases for the same primitive.

explanations.a: Correct. signal() is the writable source of truth. computed() derives from signals, memoizes, and recomputes only when a tracked dependency changes AND the computed is read.
explanations.b: Wrong. computed() is lazy + memoized — it only recomputes when needed, not every CD tick.
explanations.c: Wrong. Both are zone-independent. Signals are the foundation for zoneless Angular.
explanations.d: Wrong. They serve different purposes — source vs. derived.

questions.question: Which is the recommended way to render a conditional in Angular 17+?

options.text:
@if (user) {

{{ user.name }}

} @else {

Loading…

}
<ng-container *ngIf="user; else loading">…</ng-container>
…
<ng-template [ngTemplateOutlet]="user ? a : b">…</ng-template>

explanations.a: Correct. The new built-in @if block is the recommended approach from Angular 17+. It's faster, has better type narrowing, and doesn't need a directive import.
explanations.b: Still works but deprecated path. *ngIf requires CommonModule and produces worse type narrowing than @if.
explanations.c: Wrong. [hidden] only toggles display; the component is still constructed and bindings still run.
explanations.d: Overcomplicated. ngTemplateOutlet is for dynamic templates, not basic conditionals.

questions.question: Inside an effect(), you want to write to a signal. What's the correct take?

options.text: It's fine — effects can freely write to signals.
By default it's disallowed; you can pass { allowSignalWrites: true } as an escape hatch, but it's usually a sign the logic belongs in a computed().
Wrap the write in queueMicrotask() to avoid the loop.
Effects cannot interact with signals at all — only RxJS streams.

explanations.a: Wrong. Writing to a signal that the effect reads creates an infinite loop. Default behavior throws.
explanations.b: Correct. allowSignalWrites is intentionally an escape hatch. Most cases are better modeled as computed() (derivation) — effects are for side effects like DOM/localStorage/analytics.
explanations.c: Wrong. queueMicrotask doesn't change Angular's tracking — the loop still triggers.
explanations.d: Wrong. Effects can read signals and (with the flag) write.

questions.question: When should you reach for @defer in an Angular 17+ template?

options.text: To lazy-load a heavy child component or area based on triggers (viewport, idle, interaction, timer).
To replace lazy-loaded routes.
To debounce signal reads in the template.
To run code outside Angular zone.

explanations.a: Correct. @defer is a template-level lazy boundary with built-in triggers (on viewport, on idle, on interaction, on timer, on hover) plus @placeholder/@loading/@error blocks. Great for below-the-fold widgets, modal contents, charts.
explanations.b: Wrong. Route-level lazy loading is still done via the router's loadComponent / loadChildren.
explanations.c: Wrong. That's not its purpose. Use untracked() if you need to read without tracking.
explanations.d: Wrong. That's NgZone.runOutsideAngular().

questions.question: In a standalone Angular component, what's the preferred way to get a service?

options.text: private svc = inject(MyService);
constructor(private svc: MyService) {}
static get svc() { return Injector.get(MyService); }
Manually import a singleton from the service file.

explanations.a: Correct. inject() is the modern, ergonomic API. Works in constructors, factories, route guards (functional), interceptors (functional). Required outside class context.
explanations.b: Still works and isn't going away, but inject() is preferred for new code, especially with functional guards/resolvers/interceptors.
explanations.c: Wrong. Bypasses DI context — breaks hierarchical injection.
explanations.d: Wrong. Breaks DI entirely; service won't have its dependencies wired.

questions.question: What's the modern Angular way to auto-unsubscribe an Observable inside a component?

options.text: obs$.pipe(takeUntilDestroyed()).subscribe(…) — uses the component's DestroyRef.
ngOnDestroy() { this.sub.unsubscribe(); } — only old way still allowed.
Subject + takeUntil(destroy$.next() in ngOnDestroy).
Just leave it; Angular auto-cleans subscriptions in OnPush components.

explanations.a: Correct. takeUntilDestroyed() reads DestroyRef from the injection context, cleans up automatically on component destroy. Best modern pattern. Pass an explicit DestroyRef if called outside injection context.
explanations.b: Works but verbose and error-prone — easy to miss a sub.
explanations.c: The pre-DestroyRef boilerplate. Still works but unnecessary.
explanations.d: Wrong. Angular never auto-cleans your subscriptions. OnPush is about CD, not subs.

questions.question: You handle a search input. Each keystroke fires an HTTP request. Which operator best prevents stale responses from overwriting newer ones?

options.text: mergeMap
switchMap
concatMap
exhaustMap

explanations.a: Wrong. mergeMap runs all inner observables in parallel — old responses can land after new ones.
explanations.b: Correct. switchMap cancels the previous inner observable when a new value arrives — exactly right for search-as-you-type.
explanations.c: Wrong. concatMap queues — keystrokes pile up, stale results show in order.
explanations.d: Wrong. exhaustMap ignores new values while one is in flight — you'd drop the latest search.

questions.question: A new subscriber should receive the LAST value emitted (and only the last) and all future values. Which subject fits best?

options.text: Subject
BehaviorSubject
ReplaySubject(10)
AsyncSubject

explanations.a: Wrong. Subject doesn't replay anything; subscribers only see values emitted AFTER they subscribe.
explanations.b: Correct. BehaviorSubject holds a single current value, replays it to new subscribers, and emits future values. Requires an initial value.
explanations.c: Replays the last 10 values, not just 1. Works but overkill if you only want the last.
explanations.d: Wrong. AsyncSubject emits only the FINAL value, and only on complete().

questions.question: You have an HTTP call shared across 3 components. Without proper multicasting, what happens?

options.text: Angular's HttpClient auto-caches identical GETs.
Each subscribe triggers a fresh HTTP request — the request is made 3 times.
Only one request fires; Observable subscriptions are inherently shared.
The request fires once; subsequent subs receive a cached null.

explanations.a: Wrong. HttpClient does NOT auto-cache. You wire caching manually (shareReplay, custom interceptor, library).
explanations.b: Correct. HttpClient returns a cold Observable — every subscribe re-runs it. Solution: pipe(shareReplay({ bufferSize: 1, refCount: true })).
explanations.c: Wrong. Most observables are cold by default. Subject family + share* operators make them hot.
explanations.d: Wrong. Each sub gets its own fresh execution, not cached null.

questions.question: Given signals now exist in Angular, when do you still reach for RxJS?

options.text: Never — replace all RxJS with signals.
For event streams, HTTP, debouncing, complex async coordination (switchMap, concat, etc.). Use toSignal()/toObservable() at the boundary.
Only inside services; never in components.
Only when targeting older browsers without Proxy support.

explanations.a: Wrong. RxJS still owns time-based and event-coordination problems. Signals replaced ad-hoc state, not streams.
explanations.b: Correct. RxJS is best for streams/async coordination; signals are best for synchronous reactive state. toSignal/toObservable bridge them.
explanations.c: Wrong. There's no service-vs-component rule. Use the right tool per problem.
explanations.d: Wrong. Both work everywhere modern Angular runs.

questions.question: With ChangeDetectionStrategy.OnPush, which of these does NOT mark the component dirty?

options.text: An @Input reference changes.
An event binding in the template fires.
An async pipe emits a new value.
Mutating an object passed as @Input without changing its reference.

explanations.a: Marks dirty. New reference flowing in triggers CD on the next tick.
explanations.b: Marks dirty. Event bindings always mark the component (and ancestors).
explanations.c: Marks dirty. async pipe calls markForCheck() internally.
explanations.d: Correct. OnPush uses reference equality. Mutating in place keeps the same reference — Angular won't notice. Use a new object/array, or signals.

questions.question: In a zoneless Angular app, which will NOT trigger change detection?

options.text: A signal read in the template being updated via set()
A (click) event handler firing
A setTimeout callback mutating a non-signal class property used in the template
An async pipe receiving a new value

explanations.a: Triggers CD — signals are the primary CD trigger in zoneless, with view-level granularity.
explanations.b: Triggers CD — event bindings still schedule CD.
explanations.c: Correct. Without Zone.js patching, setTimeout doesn't notify Angular. Mutating a plain property isn't picked up — convert to a signal or use markForCheck().
explanations.d: Triggers CD — async pipe calls markForCheck() on emission.

questions.question: Angular's full-app hydration (since v16, improved in v17-19) primarily helps which Core Web Vital?

options.text: LCP — by avoiding re-render flash and reusing server-rendered DOM.
CLS — by deferring layout until JS runs.
INP — only; LCP is unaffected.
None — hydration is purely about SEO.

explanations.a: Correct. Without hydration, Angular discards SSR DOM and re-renders on bootstrap (LCP flash + CLS). With hydration, Angular claims the existing DOM. INP also benefits (event replay since v18).
explanations.b: Partially true but the primary benefit framed in Angular docs and the visible user impact is LCP. Hydration also reduces CLS.
explanations.c: Wrong. LCP is the most visible win.
explanations.d: Wrong. SEO is helped by SSR; hydration is a runtime perf concern.

questions.question: Design: a dashboard renders 50 independent widgets (charts, tables, KPIs). Each polls data on its own cadence. Users drag/resize/rearrange. Currently CD runs on every widget every tick and the page is sluggish. What's your architecture?
model_answer_key_points: OnPush everywhere as the default — sibling widgets don't trigger each other's CD
Per-widget standalone component with @Input config; data comes from a widget-scoped data service (providers on the component, not root)
Signal-based reactive state per widget for view-level dirty marking
Virtualization for off-screen widgets — IntersectionObserver to pause polling + skip render
Polling via interval() + switchMap() + takeUntilDestroyed (auto-cleanup)
Drag/resize via CDK Drag-Drop; persist layout as a signal store, debounced write
Profile first (Angular DevTools, Performance panel) before reaching for Web Workers
Heavy chart computation in a Web Worker via Comlink only if profiling proves it dominates

questions.question: @ngrx/signals SignalStore is best described as:

options.text: A drop-in replacement for the Redux pattern with the same boilerplate.
A lightweight reactive store built on signals with features (withState, withMethods, withComputed, withHooks) — far less boilerplate than classic NgRx.
A wrapper around BehaviorSubject with selectors.
Identical to NGXS but with a different name.

explanations.a: Wrong. The whole point is escaping Redux ceremony (actions/reducers/effects) when you don't need it.
explanations.b: Correct. SignalStore composes features. Reactive via signals, no actions/reducers, optional rxMethod for async. Ergonomic for medium-sized state.
explanations.c: Wrong. It's built on signals + computed, not BehaviorSubject.
explanations.d: Wrong. NGXS uses Redux pattern with decorators.

questions.question: You're building a dashboard that mostly READS data from APIs with some caching and refetch needs. NgRx vs SignalStore vs TanStack Query (Angular). Best pick?

options.text: Classic NgRx — gives you full audit trail.
TanStack Query (Angular adapter) for server state + SignalStore (or just signals) for client UI state.
Build it on raw RxJS subjects.
Skip state management; component-local state for everything.

explanations.a: Possible but heavy. Most dashboards don't need the audit-trail rigor; you'll write a lot of boilerplate for caching that TanStack gives free.
explanations.b: Correct. The modern split — server state (caching, refetch, stale-while-revalidate) belongs in a query library; client UI state (open/closed, filters) belongs in signals/SignalStore.
explanations.c: Works but you'll reinvent caching, dedup, retry, error handling.
explanations.d: Wrong. Cross-component shared state quickly becomes a tangled prop chain.

questions.question: In NgRx, what's the main reason to use createSelector instead of mapping inside a component?

options.text: It's faster to write.
It memoizes by input reference — recomputes only when inputs change, even if the parent state slice changes elsewhere.
Selectors auto-subscribe components to the store.
Selectors run in a Web Worker.

explanations.a: Wrong. They're not faster to write; the benefit is runtime.
explanations.b: Correct. Memoization is the killer feature — expensive derived state isn't recomputed on every store emission, only when the selected slices change by reference.
explanations.c: Wrong. You still need store.select(selector) and subscribe (or async pipe).
explanations.d: Wrong. Selectors run on the main thread.

questions.question: Strictly Typed Reactive Forms (Angular 14+) give you what main benefit?

options.text: Compile-time type safety on form values, controls.get('name'), .value, etc.
Automatic form validation.
Smaller bundle size.
Built-in async validators.

explanations.a: Correct. The value shape is inferred from the FormGroup definition. Typos in get('name') and wrong .value usage become compile errors.
explanations.b: Wrong. You still wire validators yourself.
explanations.c: Negligible bundle effect.
explanations.d: Wrong. Async validators have existed for ages, not new with typed forms.

questions.question: You're building a custom input component you want to use with reactive forms. What do you need?

options.text: Implement ControlValueAccessor + provide NG_VALUE_ACCESSOR with multi: true.
Implement OnChanges and emit valueChange events.
Extend FormControl directly.
Use ng-content to project the input from the parent form.

explanations.a: Correct. CVA is the contract: writeValue, registerOnChange, registerOnTouched, setDisabledState. Then provide NG_VALUE_ACCESSOR with multi: true so multiple CVAs can coexist.
explanations.b: Works for two-way binding via banana-in-a-box but doesn't integrate with reactive form state (touched, dirty, errors).
explanations.c: Wrong. FormControl isn't designed to be extended for UI.
explanations.d: Wrong. Projection skips the encapsulation and doesn't expose the custom UI.

questions.question: Your async validator checks username availability via HTTP. The user types fast. What's the most likely bug if you just call http.get inside the validator function?

options.text: Validator runs on every keystroke; old responses can resolve after new ones, causing wrong validation state.
Angular blocks the form until the request completes.
Form values get sent in the request body automatically.
Validators can't return Observables — only Promises.

explanations.a: Correct. Async validators face the same race problem as search-as-you-type. Use a debounceTime + switchMap inside the validator, or check 'this is the latest value' before applying.
explanations.b: Wrong. The form just stays in PENDING state during the async validator.
explanations.c: Wrong. You construct the request manually.
explanations.d: Wrong. Both Observables and Promises are supported.

questions.question: What's the main benefit of `satisfies` over a type annotation?

options.text: It's a runtime check in production.
It validates a value against a type while preserving the narrowest inferred type of the value.
It enforces immutability.
It removes the need for generics.

explanations.a: Wrong. satisfies is compile-time only.
explanations.b: Correct. The value keeps its literal/inferred shape so property access stays specific; you still get the type-conformance guarantee. Type annotations widen the type.
explanations.c: Wrong. That's `as const`.
explanations.d: Wrong. Generics and satisfies solve different problems.

questions.question: You have `type Result = { ok: true; data: User } | { ok: false; error: string }`. Best way to narrow?

options.text: Check r.ok with an if; TS narrows via the discriminant property.
Use typeof r.data === 'object'.
Use instanceof Result.
Cast: (r as { data: User }).data.

explanations.a: Correct. Discriminated unions are the idiomatic pattern. `if (r.ok) { r.data }` narrows the type because TS sees the literal discriminant.
explanations.b: Works for simple cases but doesn't scale and loses the discriminant pattern.
explanations.c: Wrong. Result is a type, not a class — instanceof doesn't apply.
explanations.d: Wrong. Casting bypasses safety; defeats the point of unions.

questions.question: Given `type User = { id: number; name: string; email: string }`, which gives you `{ name: string; email: string }`?

options.text: Pick<User, 'name' | 'email'>
Omit<User, 'name' | 'email'>
Partial<User>
Record<'name' | 'email', User>

explanations.a: Correct. Pick selects the listed keys.
explanations.b: Wrong. Omit removes the listed keys (would give just { id }).
explanations.c: Wrong. Partial makes all props optional.
explanations.d: Wrong. Record creates a map type from keys to a value type.

questions.question: What does `as const` do to `const colors = ['red','green','blue'] as const;`?

options.text: Marks the array as readonly and narrows element types to literals ('red' | 'green' | 'blue').
Freezes the array at runtime via Object.freeze.
Makes the array reactive.
Forces the variable into the const enum optimization.

explanations.a: Correct. The array becomes readonly and element types narrow from string to the exact literal union — perfect for deriving a union type via colors[number].
explanations.b: Wrong. No runtime change — TS-only.
explanations.c: Wrong. TS has no notion of reactivity.
explanations.d: Wrong. Const enums are a separate language feature.

questions.question: Container queries differ from media queries in that they:

options.text: Style based on the size of a parent container, not the viewport.
Only work in print mode.
Replace flexbox.
Are a JavaScript API only.

explanations.a: Correct. Set container-type: inline-size; on a parent, then @container (min-width: 400px) {…} styles child elements based on the container's width — perfect for reusable components placed in varying layouts.
explanations.b: Wrong.
explanations.c: Wrong. Different concern entirely.
explanations.d: Wrong. Pure CSS, baseline in all modern browsers.

questions.question: User opens a modal via a button click. After close, focus should:

options.text: Move to the body — let the browser decide.
Return to the button that opened the modal.
Move to the first focusable element on the page.
Be left wherever the user clicked.

explanations.a: Wrong. Body focus disorients keyboard and screen reader users.
explanations.b: Correct. WAI-ARIA Authoring Practices: focus returns to the triggering element after a modal closes. Keep a reference to the trigger when opening.
explanations.c: Wrong. Surprises the user; they lose context.
explanations.d: Wrong. Predictable focus behavior is required for keyboard a11y.

questions.question: What does this select: `.card:has(.featured)` ?

options.text: Cards that contain a descendant with class 'featured' — selects the parent based on its child.
Cards inside a 'featured' container.
Any element with class 'featured' nested under .card.
Invalid CSS.

explanations.a: Correct. :has() is the long-awaited parent selector — selects elements that CONTAIN a matching descendant. Baseline in all modern browsers (2023+).
explanations.b: Wrong. That's `.featured .card`.
explanations.c: Wrong. That's `.card .featured`.
explanations.d: Wrong. Valid and widely supported.

questions.question: Webpack Module Federation in a micro-frontend setup mainly solves which problem?

options.text: Sharing code (components, libs) between independently deployed apps at runtime.
Replacing service workers.
Bundling CSS only.
It's a build-time-only feature with no runtime impact.

explanations.a: Correct. Module Federation exposes/consumes modules across remotes at runtime — one app can lazy-load components from another deployed app, share Angular/RxJS instances via singleton-shared deps.
explanations.b: Wrong. Different domain.
explanations.c: Wrong. Cross-app JS module sharing is the headline.
explanations.d: Wrong. Federation is fundamentally runtime.

questions.question: When would you pick SSR vs SSG vs ISR (or Angular's prerender) for a content-heavy site with some authenticated areas?
model_answer_key_points: SSG / prerender — public marketing pages that change rarely (best LCP, cheap to serve from CDN)
SSR — pages with per-request personalization or fresh data (auth-aware, dashboard landing)
ISR / scheduled rebuild — content that updates predictably (blog posts, product pages) — get SSG speed with periodic freshness
Client-side only — deeply interactive logged-in app shell after a fast initial route
Use Angular's prerender for SSG-ish pages, full SSR for dynamic ones, and consider edge runtimes for latency
Tradeoff to mention: SSR cost (compute per request), SSG cost (build time + storage), ISR cost (cache complexity)

questions.question: Main advantage of Nx for a multi-app Angular workspace?

options.text: Computation cache + affected detection — only rebuild/test what changed, share results across team and CI.
Replaces Angular CLI entirely; you can't use ng commands anymore.
Mandatory micro-frontend architecture.
Auto-deploys to AWS.

explanations.a: Correct. Nx's task graph + local & remote (Nx Cloud) cache makes large repos fast. nx affected:test runs only what's touched by a diff.
explanations.b: Wrong. Nx extends, not replaces. ng commands still work; Nx adds generators, executors, and the graph.
explanations.c: Wrong. Nx supports micro-FE but doesn't require it.
explanations.d: Wrong.

questions.question: Look at the snippet. What's the worst problem with this code?

options.text: Missing trackBy on the *ngFor.
Nested subscribes leak both subscriptions and create a race between param changes.
It should use async/await instead.
The component should be OnPush.

explanations.a: A perf concern, not a correctness bug. The leak + race is worse.
explanations.b: Correct. Neither subscription is unsubscribed (leak). And id=1 response may arrive AFTER id=2, overwriting correct data. Use switchMap + takeUntilDestroyed, or toSignal().
explanations.c: Wrong. async/await doesn't fix either issue; RxJS operators are the idiomatic answer.
explanations.d: Useful but unrelated to the bugs.

questions.question: What happens with this code?

options.text: Logs 0 once, nothing else.
Throws — by default effects can't write to signals they read; infinite loop guard kicks in.
Increments forever until the browser hangs.
Logs 0 then 1, then stops.

explanations.a: Wrong. The effect would re-run as count changes — but the runtime detects the loop.
explanations.b: Correct. Writing to a signal an effect reads is disallowed by default. Either restructure as a computed(), or pass { allowSignalWrites: true } if you really need it (rare).
explanations.c: Wrong — the loop detection prevents this.
explanations.d: Wrong.

questions.question: Component is OnPush. Why doesn't this update the view?

options.text: OnPush uses reference equality on @Input. Mutating user.name keeps the same reference — Angular doesn't notice. Need a new object or signal.
user.name is readonly by default.
Inputs are immutable in Angular.
Need to call cdr.detach() first.

explanations.a: Correct. OnPush + reference mutation is the most common 'why isn't it updating' bug. Fix: replace user with a new object, use signals, or call markForCheck after the mutation (least elegant).
explanations.b: Wrong. user.name is freely mutable in TS unless typed readonly.
explanations.c: Wrong. Inputs are regular properties.
explanations.d: Wrong. detach() stops CD entirely — opposite of what you want.

questions.question: Why does this cause excessive HTTP calls?

options.text: Every keystroke fires a POST; needs debounceTime + distinctUntilChanged + switchMap for cancellation.
valueChanges only fires on blur.
Angular auto-debounces it; problem is server-side.
The form is missing FormsModule.

explanations.a: Correct. valueChanges emits on every change; no debounce. Nested subscribe also leaks and races. Use pipe(debounceTime(300), distinctUntilChanged(), switchMap(v => http.post(...))) with takeUntilDestroyed.
explanations.b: Wrong. valueChanges emits on every value change.
explanations.c: Wrong. Angular does not auto-debounce.
explanations.d: Unrelated to this bug.

questions.question: Tell me about the most complex Angular UI you've built. What made it complex, what trade-offs did you make, and what would you do differently today?
questions.model_answer_structure: STAR: Situation (1-2 sentences), Task (your role), Action (3-5 concrete decisions with reasoning), Result (measurable outcome), Reflection (what you'd change today).
model_answer_key_points: Picked a genuinely complex project (real scope, not a toy)
Named the dimension of complexity (scale, coupling, perf, coordination, deadline pressure)
Concrete trade-offs with reasoning ('we chose X over Y because…')
At least one quantifiable outcome (perf number, ship date hit, adoption metric)
Reflection — what you'd change with current Angular knowledge (signals, zoneless, control flow)
Avoid hero narrative — credit collaborators where real

questions.question: Tell me about a time you disagreed with a designer or PM about a UI decision. How did you resolve it?
questions.model_answer_structure: STAR with emphasis on Action and Result. Show empathy, not opposition.
model_answer_key_points: Frame the disagreement around USER impact, not personal preference
Show you sought to understand their reasoning before pushing your view
Concrete way you brought data (perf metric, a11y constraint, dev cost, user research)
Outcome that respected both sides — compromise or new shared understanding, not 'I won'
If you lost the argument, explain how you supported the decision afterward (key senior signal)

questions.question: Tell me about a time you fixed a serious frontend performance problem in production.
questions.model_answer_structure: STAR with strong focus on investigation method, not just the fix.
model_answer_key_points: Specific symptom (slow page, high INP/LCP, memory growth)
Investigation method — how you measured (DevTools, Lighthouse, profiler, RUM)
Root cause clearly named
Fix with reasoning + before/after metric
Prevention — what you added to catch it next time (perf budget, CI check, monitoring)
Avoid 'we rewrote everything' answers — interviewers want surgical fixes

questions.question: You're refactoring a large Angular component with AI assistance. Which workflow gives the best result for production code?

options.text: Accept all AI suggestions inline and run tests at the end.
Ask the AI to refactor, review the diff, run unit tests + type check, and gate commits on green CI.
Let the AI auto-commit each suggestion to keep velocity high.
Disable type checking during the refactor for speed; re-enable at the end.

explanations.a: Wrong. Accepting inline without diff review is how subtle regressions land. AI output requires the same review discipline as any contributor.
explanations.b: Correct. AI proposes, you review the diff, the type system + tests + CI gate the change. Treat AI like a fast intern, not a senior.
explanations.c: Wrong. Auto-commit removes the review step that catches hallucinations.
explanations.d: Wrong. The type system is your strongest signal that an AI refactor is incorrect.

questions.question: In Cursor / similar AI IDEs, what's the most useful action when the AI keeps suggesting outdated API patterns (e.g. *ngIf instead of @if)?

options.text: Add a rules file (e.g. .cursorrules) that pins the codebase's modern patterns and Angular version.
Switch IDEs.
Manually delete the suggestion every time.
Disable AI completions entirely.

explanations.a: Correct. Rules files (.cursorrules, .clinerules, CLAUDE.md, etc.) let you bias the model toward your stack/version/preferences without re-prompting every time. Highest leverage.
explanations.b: The same problem exists in any AI IDE.
explanations.c: Painful and doesn't scale.
explanations.d: Throws the baby out with the bathwater.

questions.question: Tools like CodeRabbit / Greptile add AI to PR review. What's the most realistic productivity benefit in 2025?

options.text: Replaces human reviewers entirely.
Catches obvious bugs (null checks, off-by-one, security smells) and surfaces context, so human reviewers focus on architecture and intent.
Auto-merges PRs that pass tests.
Generates new features from the PR description.

explanations.a: Wrong. They miss intent, business context, and tribal knowledge. Best as a first-pass review.
explanations.b: Correct. The realistic value: filter the low-hanging stuff so humans spend their attention where it matters.
explanations.c: Not yet mainstream — and a bad idea without strong test coverage.
explanations.d: That's a different category of tool (codegen).

questions.question: Top failure mode of GitHub Copilot / Cursor when writing Angular code (in your experience as the reviewer):

options.text: Hallucinates Angular APIs that don't exist or are deprecated (often confidently).
Refuses to write TypeScript.
Only suggests Vue components.
Can't write tests at all.

explanations.a: Correct. The classic failure: invented operators, wrong RxJS signatures, deprecated decorators, *ngFor over the new @for, old form APIs. Always verify against Angular's current docs.
explanations.b: Wrong. TS is its strongest mode.
explanations.c: Wrong. Suggests Angular fine when the file context is Angular.
explanations.d: Wrong. Tests are often the easiest output.
